summary: This lesson covers how go verbs form informal positive commands by simply dropping the -go (tengo → ten, vengo → ven, salgo → sal, digo → di). It contrasts this with formal commands that use the -ga subjunctive form. Object pronoun placement with commands is also practiced.

teachings.summary: For informal positive commands, go verbs simply drop the -go ending: tengo → ten, vengo → ven, salgo → sal, digo → di, pongo → pon. This is different from regular verbs which drop the -s from the tú form.
teachings.rule: Informal positive command of go verbs: drop -go. Tengo → ten. Vengo → ven. Salgo → sal. Digo → di. Formal commands still use -ga form.
teachings.notes: Dime and dígame are both heard when answering the phone in Spain. Venga has lost its formal feeling in Spain — it's used colloquially as 'come on' by everyone.

teachings.summary: Go verbs use -ga as the subjunctive base. Practiced with commands (formal uses subjunctive), negative commands, and after cuando referring to the future.

examples.english: Tell me when you come (future reference)
Tell me when you do it (formal, future reference)

teachings.summary: With positive commands, object pronouns attach to the end of the verb. With negative commands, pronouns go before the verb.
teachings.rule: Positive command: verb + pronoun (póngalo). Negative command: no + pronoun + verb (no lo ponga).
